column2: The set of tasks a healthcare worker is legally and professionally permitted to perform, based on their license, certification, and training.
column4: A CNA's scope of practice includes taking vital signs and assisting with bathing, but not administering IV medications.

column2: The process by which a licensed professional assigns a task within their own scope to someone with a narrower scope, while keeping responsibility for the outcome.
column4: An RN using delegation asks the CNA to record intake and output, but stays accountable for checking the numbers and acting on them.

column2: The structured transfer of responsibility for a patient from one clinician or shift to another, including the current plan, recent changes, and what to watch for.
column4: A handoff between the night nurse and the day nurse is where most missed information turns into a near-miss.

column2: A standardized handoff format — Situation, Background, Assessment, Recommendation — used to communicate a patient's status concisely between team members.
column4: Using SBAR, the nurse tells the physician the situation, the background, her assessment, and what she recommends doing next.

column2: A group of healthcare workers from different professions who coordinate care for the same patient, each contributing expertise from their own scope.
column4: An interprofessional team for a stroke patient may include a physician, an RN, a physical therapist, a speech-language pathologist, a pharmacist, and a social worker.

column2: The cluster of healthcare professions outside nursing, medicine, and dentistry — including therapists, technologists, technicians, and pharmacists — that provide diagnostic, technical, therapeutic, and support services.
column4: Allied health roles such as respiratory therapists and medical laboratory scientists outnumber physicians in most hospitals.

column2: A clinician — typically a nurse practitioner or physician assistant — trained to diagnose, prescribe, and manage care, often working alongside or in place of a physician.
column4: In many primary-care clinics, an advanced practice provider sees patients independently for routine visits and consults the physician on complex cases.

column2: A working relationship in which a more-licensed clinician oversees the practice of another team member, ranging from on-site review of every case to remote collaboration.
column4: A PA practices under physician supervision, though the exact rules for how close that supervision must be vary by state.

column2: Authorized by a state board to practice a profession after meeting education, examination, and sometimes experience requirements; losing a license removes legal authority to practice.
column4: A licensed RN can administer medications that a certified medical assistant cannot, even if both have been on the unit for the same number of years.

column2: Recognized by a professional body or state registry as having met a training or testing standard for a specific role, usually narrower than full licensure.
column4: A certified nursing assistant has completed state-approved training and passed a competency exam, but does not hold a nursing license.

column2: Registered nurse — a licensed nurse who assesses patients, plans and delivers care, administers medications, and delegates tasks to CNAs and LPNs; prepared via ADN or BSN plus the NCLEX-RN exam.
column4: The RN on the unit assigns herself the new admission and delegates vital-sign rounds to the CNA.

column2: Doctor of medicine — a physician licensed to diagnose disease, prescribe treatment, and perform procedures within their specialty after medical school and residency.
column4: The attending MD writes the discharge plan after rounding with the interprofessional team.

column2: Physician assistant — a licensed clinician who diagnoses, prescribes, and performs many procedures under a collaborative relationship with a physician.
column4: In the emergency department, a PA may run the low-acuity side of the board while the physician handles trauma.
